Sentence: You wake before the light, as you always do — not from an alarm but from a small, mechanical insistence at the edge of your sleep.
The tide sensors have a nervous rhythm tonight.
It threads through the quiet like a reminder you can't ignore.
an anchor you loop into habit more than time.
Outside, the horizon is a thin gray seam; there is a wrongness to it you know without naming.
The low tide sits higher than it should, and
that higher notch cut into the cliff — a new mouth in the bluff — feels like an accusation.
You move through the station with the slow efficiency of someone cataloguing small defeats.
The tide gauges blink an uptick in numbers that don't belong.
A cluster of sensors reports minor structural flex where there should
sharp spikes, then a slow tail.
She left it mid-stream — the tablet's carrier app glowing like a small, urgent
pulse.
one small house lost, one more notch in
the cliff, a co-op shed full of nets drenched in silt.
You count them as if they were numbers on a grant form, because the grants will be the ledger you live under for the next
year.
The tally curves back to a single, sharp memory — the house you lost in the family flood.
The guilt is a folded thing at the base of your throat you smooth without thinking.
where the dunes were, he says, the grass used to stand like a fence.
His stories don't comfort so much as adjust the tilt of
they are not simply rocks and sand, child.
They were a way to hold back what wants to come in.
Nyla appears from the alcove with a thermos and a scowl that softens when she sees you watching the stream of numbers.
She's already in motion — fingers quick on the tablet, breath a little foggy in the cold.
You're up early.
The sensors haven't quieted since midnight.
Look at this.
She holds the tablet toward you, the graph jagging like a heartbeat.
I saw the notch on the bluff.
Are these spikes correlated to the gust front last night?
Partly.
There's that.
But the waveform doesn't match the usual recipe.
There's energy in frequencies we haven't been seeing on the low tide.
Either there's an underwater reflection pattern changing — or we've got shifting bathymetry near the quay.
She taps the screen; her tone is brisk but small tremors show.
Shifting bottom would explain the lean-to.
It would explain the nets.
How quickly does that escalate?
Not instantly.
It's an edge.
But edges are where things start to pick up.
If the bar moves, the waves refract differently.
If the bar moves toward the shore, every low tide is effectively a little higher.
And that matches the gauges.
The uptick on the tide log is real.
Real and boringly persistent.
She offers a crooked, tired smile.
'Mayor Hale pinged me at three.
Emergency meeting at ten.
Everyone's been told.'
calendars, grant windows, investors' latent interest.
The corporate offer — Elena Park's firm — hangs at the edges of your inbox like a sleek shadow.
Her proposal: quick engineered defenses, a
seawall the books will like.
It reads on the screen as certainty.
It reads in the mayor's curt line as pressure.
nature-first, community-led, living shorelines.
You will have to make chips of time add up to protection.
follow the numbers first
You lean close to the console, fingers hovering over data sets — let the graphs and the models speak before you let the story speak.
You tell Nyla to log everything and prepare the visualization for Hale.
go to the quay to see with your own eyes
You close the tablet, slide on your boots, and decide you need the smell of wet rope and the tactile truth of the quay more than another graph.
Nyla protests for a second but hands you a thermal flask.
Continue to the quay scene
The quay smells of wet canvas, diesel, and something older — cedar oil and fish breath.
The lean-to sits as a wounded thing, half-swallowed by sand and silt, the patchwork of its canvas like a bandage
at half-mast.
You walk the spray-slick boards carefully, boots making soft thunks.
Each step is a small report.
Aiden Koa is there, shoulders squared against wind, hands on a rope as if steadying more than a boat.
He looks up when you approach, and for a moment the sea in his eyes is the
steady, tired, warm.
He doesn't come forward; instead he waits for you to close the space.
Did you get Nyla's graph?
This morning the shoreline ran my hand like a different thing.
I saw it.
The lean-to didn't stand a chance if the bar shifted.
How's the co-op shed?
Mostly nets.
A few boxes of bait we can save.
Old man Jiro's traps are gone, though.
He'd kill the sea if he could and pay nothing for it.
He smirks, then goes quiet.
'Hale sent word for ten.
Elena's people were on the line early.
She's got numbers and a truck.
I don't like the idea of 'safety' being a concrete contract.'
Safety isn't a single shape.
I want to protect what we have without carving the sea off from us.
Aiden Koa's jaw tightens in a way that means more is unsaid.
He looks toward the cliffs — toward the new notch — then back at you.
You always say that.
Living shorelines and dunes and all.
I— I get it.
My mother remembers where she used to gather clams as a child.
If a wall goes up, those places die.
He laughs, brittle.
'Maybe you and Elena can engineer the moon together.
Will that bring back a beach to walk on with bare feet?'
I don't want poetry to take precedent over people.
I want both.
I want options that don't cost us the things we love.
And what if there isn't time for both?
What if the grant timeline forces a faster fix?
Then we make the fastest fix the least destructive one.
If that's impossible, then we choose what we can live with after the flood.
You carry so much on your back, Maya.
Sometimes I wonder if you believe you can fix everything yourself.
I don't believe I'm invincible.
I believe in doing better with what we have.
And I believe in the people who show up.
Nyla calls from the boardwalk, voice distant but steady — a conduit of data and practical urgency.
Mayor wants us at the hall.
He said bring what we have and nothing theatrical.
Keep it simple.
fence the dune, he said, plant the roots where they will hold.
You memorize the cadence like a prescription.
reach out before you go in
don't lose yourself in the fight.
tuck the tide-watch deeper into your palm
I'm here, but I'm not stopping you from fighting alone.
Continue to the boardwalk & marsh scene
The boardwalk feels like a place of holdings and losses — a liminal theatre between land and water.
Puddles mirror the bleak sky in small, reflecting patient rounds.
Nyla's tablet is back in your hands, displaying
a time-lapse of the bar movement over the last two months.
The image is quiet and terrible in its steadiness: a slow migration, pixels shifting shoreward.
Ten a.m., community hall.
Emergency.
He writes in his practical, debtor's hand.
You can almost see him arranging budgets and cross-checking re-election lines on a mental ledger.
The wording is careful; the pressure is not.
You press the tide-watch into your palm and feel the brass warmth and the tiny dent where your fingernail hooked it once as a child.
You steady yourself on that weight and on the memory of
Old Man Cala's dunes.
You are tired in a way that something in you calls productive; you are also afraid because every small failure has felt like a vote against you.
The grant timeline waits like
a metronome; Elena's offer waits like a polished coin.
Somewhere beneath all of this is the people you love — fishermen, grandmothers, the kid who runs the fish stall — and the thought of losing them
to the wrong version of 'safety' is a hollow ache.
You take one last look back at the water.
The sea is indifferent and intimate at once, pressing the future against the town's heels.
You steel yourself, as you have steeled a hundred times, to go
the living shorelines, the dunes, the marshes.
You habitually rehearse your line about community-led defenses in the quiet between waves and data, and the rehearsal makes
the pitch both less miraculous and more necessary.
You walk toward the path that climbs to the community hall.
The bulbs strung outside are not yet lit.
Your steps are measured.
Your breath makes a small fog.
In the pocket of your parka the tide-watch rests like a small truth.
grant officers, anxious homeowners, Elena's envoy, Mayor Hale with his folders.
You know there will be moments where a single phrase can shift the room.
You prepare the phrase in your head — succinct, unflashy, stubborn in principle.
the sound of the town seems to gather behind the door, the weight of Old Man Cala's stories and the lean-to's absence settling like dust
on your shoulders.
You press your thumb against the tide-watch and let the brass warmth remind you of why you wake before dawn.
You inhale.
You push the door open.
You step inside, and the room turns toward you with the gravity of small, human needs.
You can already feel the shape of the debate ready to form — the way the word 'safety' will be
wielded like a tool.
You have minutes to make a case, seconds to catch a look, a pause to let a single sentence land.
You steel yourself to speak before the room decides.
coffee, salt-stung sweat, and the faint metallic tang of too many handheld radios.
Your field notebook lies open on the nearest table like a set
of prayers — living-dune cross sections, marsh restoration timelines, volunteer rosters, sketches of planted berms.
The pages smell faintly of damp paper and your thumb-smudged graphite.
Mayor Jerome Hale moves through the room, a damp scarf thrown around his neck, folders tucked under one arm.
He smiles with the kind of curved politeness you learned to read as 'I have to be all things to all people.' His voice reaches you when he passes.
Maya — good to see you.
We need you clear and sharp tonight.
There will be reporters on the line if Elena's backers press.
You swallow and feel the brass of the tide-watch in your pocket.
The warmth is small and private; it steadies a pulse that is already trying to sprint.
salt-stiff navy canvas jacket, hands stuffed in patched oilskins, eyes moving across the room until they find you.
He gives you a look—protective, impatient, two syllables of warning without
sound.
You catch the lift of his mouth as he nods once, and that little motion steadies you more than the watch does.
You look like you rehearsed the whole walk over, Serrin.
Don't let them steamroll the people.
what will it mean if the seawall goes up; who will lose access to the quay; which nets will come up empty.
Immediate, engineered barriers are the fastest way to prevent loss of life and property.
Our model is proven in other regions.
With private investment, we can have physical protection in two construction seasons.
Her cadence is crisp and hollow in the hall's warm light.
It sounds dangerously like comfort.
You feel your chest tighten; 'fast' and 'proven' rub against the lived memory of last winter's roaring flood and the mud where the Miller house used to be.
You stand and make your way to the table.

Nyla is already feeding you last-minute figures from her tablet — projected erosion rates, economic models showing the co-op's expected income over five years if the wall is built, and worst-case ecologic impacts.
If the wall cuts access at low tide, the fishers lose seasonal grounds — that's a 32 Percentage projected income drop for us in year one.
The marsh retreat accelerates erosion inland by a meter a year in some models.
the living dune you sketch, rooted with marram grass and seeded for resilience, contrasted with Elena's concrete segments.
Numbers on the tablet glow like small alarms.
Your mouth feels dry.
There is a part of you that wants to throw the tablet across the room and another that wants to show every figure until the room understands what is being traded.
• Place the cross-section at the center of the table
You set the page down, fingers splayed, and your voice finds the low register you use with the town.
'This is what will grow back if we protect it ourselves,' you say, tracing the dune line.
A murmur runs through the hall — some nod, some purse their lips.
• Lean on Nyla's tablet and challenge Elena's timeline
You lean in, tapping a projected stat.

'Two seasons is a fiction in our weather; construction on that scale will be delayed by storms, permits, and—' Elena cuts in, cool and precise, and the room tilts toward technicalities instead of losses.
You choose a tone somewhere between, because neither showy gesture nor clinical argument will settle this room tonight.
You speak, but the first few sentences come out smaller than you practiced; the room fills them so you push harder.
I am not arguing against protection.
I'm arguing for who decides what 'protection' means.
Living shorelines work with tides and species that keep our sands and reeds in place.
We can design modest, hybrid structures now and scale them — with the community maintaining access, with jobs for local fishers and volunteers.
We don't have to be forced into a single suit of armor that changes the coastline forever.
Ms.
Serrin, with all due respect, what you describe takes decades to establish at scale and depends on variable ecological responses.
People need dry houses next storm season.
My firm can secure federal matching funds if the town commits to an engineered approach today.
Elena has donors ready; matching funds make this politically palatable for the council.
I have to consider the budget and the optics.
If the grant doesn't push, we may lose both options.
The room tenses.
A woman whose porch was undercut last winter grips a thermos so hard the cardboard creaks.
Elderly Homeowner My daughter's children sleep in the spare room in the back because the front of our house leans.
What do you propose we do tonight while you figure growth plans?
Your throat closes.
You hear Old Man Cala's voice in your head — the slow cadence of someone who remembers when the estuary fed half our town, and of dunes that used to be broad and soft.
We've always learned the water.
We don't bar it and call it safe.
I am not a scientist, but I know the sea forgets concrete and remembers life.
You meet Aiden Koa's eyes and he stands up before you finish.
He moves not to the podium but to where some of the fishers sit, rolling his shoulders like someone bracing for a shove.
If you seal off access, Elena, you might keep water out of their basements — but you'll keep boats out of their hands.
You make the sea private.
How is that protecting us?
Mr.
Koa, this isn't about making the sea private.
It's about preventing catastrophe.
The design includes community ramps at scheduled intervals.
Scheduled intervals don't catch a sudden spring run.
They don't account for the old cycles we still fish.
You can't schedule a livelihood.
The back-and-forth opens like a seam.
Voices rise, not shrill but raw.
Mayor Hale tries to mediate, hands pressed together, an old habit of diplomacy.
Please — we've all heard both sides.
We can table this and revisit in the morning.
We have to finish the stakeholder conference call; there's a lot at stake.
someone starts to clap in agreement, someone else hisses in outrage.
Prayerful glances shoot toward Old Man Cala and then fall back to you, to Elena, to Jerome.
The hum of
the projector sounds suddenly intrusive, a small machinery heartbeat in a room that wants an answer like a wound wants bandaging.
Nyla leans in, urgent and clinical Maya, if we delay, erosion models show an extra—
Nyla is cut off by the Mayor raising his hand, voice thin with the strain of trying to hold multiple futures in one face.
We can't make an irreversible commitment tonight.
We will bring this to a vote at ten tomorrow.
Please, everyone — let’s reconvene then.
tomorrow.
The word becomes a taut wire stretched across the room.
It's both relief and a new kind of torture.
Delay buys time but also gives
power to the loudest, the richest, the most certain.
It births a night for second-guessing.
• Stay after to talk quietly with the fishers
You linger as people filter out; a few fishers come close, lowering their voices.
You listen more than you speak, making notes at the margins of your notebook as Nyla and Aiden trade plans.
The weight of promises presses in, but the smallness of these shared plans feels slightly like a hearth.
• Follow Elena to her projector and ask for specifics
You trail Elena to the front, forcing a technical exchange that cuts into her polished pitch.
She answers with risk numbers and contingency clauses; the language is precise and unmoved.
It feels like bargaining with steel.
You stay and collect what you can — names of people who will speak tomorrow, a list of concerns about access and livelihoods, the small, human wounds that won't read on a grant application.
Aiden and
Nyla sketch a plan to be at the quay at dawn with nets and cameras to document access points.
You scribble enrollment rosters for volunteers who will help seed dune plantings if you can buy time.
You step out into the narrow lane and inhale the night.
The boardwalk calls like a memory of wood and salt.
Old planks groan under your boots as you choose the path away from the brittle
a green-tinged mist, marsh reeds bowing like folks in prayer.
You walk slowly, the meeting replaying behind your eyes like a sequence of failed stitches.
Each exchange—Elena's polished promises, Aiden's blunt loyalty, Nyla's urgent numbers—resonates against the boards.
The tide breathes up and down; it does
not care for committees, for grants, or for reconciliations.
The future feels like a drawer pulled open to reveal not one option but a scattering of delicate, breakable pieces.
what are you willing to trade to buy someone else’s calm tonight?
Which lives are invisible if the corridor of access is sealed?
The doubt is a hard pebble lodged under your tongue.
You stop near a warped plank that used to be a favorite spot for children to fling stones.
You press your palm to the railing; it is slick with salt, the grain of the wood standing like fossilized lines of weather.
A howl of a dog far down the shore reminds you that the town is itself a living thing made of people and habits, not only of grant matrices and engineering reports.
The meeting's postponement feels
less like mercy and more like a countdown.
Tomorrow is not a reset; it is a weighting.
You think of the Miller house, of Old Man Cala's stories, of the smell of damp wool in Aiden Koa's jacket.
You think of the volunteers who will come at dawn because they still believe in
being neighbors.
You think of the models Nyla fed you, numbers that look like quiet avalanches when you arrange them against human schedules.
The sound of the tide is steady, indifferent; there is a cruelty to that steadiness.
Your chest tightens further until each breath is a manual task, deliberate and small.
You know, with the same certainty that tide charts give you, that a choice tomorrow will define whose lives count.
The night presses in.
The decision waits.
You stand where the hall narrows into the foyer, the tide-watch cold and heavy against your palm.
The room inside smells like boiled coffee and wet wool; the boardroom tables are covered in maps with salt-specked
annotations, and the air tastes faintly of iron — worry pressed into the grain of wood.
Behind you the boardwalk creaks; the town feels like a thing held together by small noises, by habits you have spent yourself trying to reckon with.
Ahead, the council is small, narrowed down to faces that
Mayor Jerome Hale at the head, Dr.
Elena Park on his right, Aiden Koa leaning against a pillar with his arms folded, Nyla tapping at a glowing tablet, and two elder fishers with
hands like carved oars.
Old Man Cala lingers in the doorway, his silhouette a weathered punctuation.
Nobody smiles.
You breathe in, and the breath comes tight — a rope pulled through a small loop.
The earlier public meeting folded into itself overnight; now the choices are surgical, and every word will be counted like
a score on a tide chart.
You can feel the room waiting like a lung.
We don't have the luxury of delay.
Investors want decisions.
People want assurances.
Let's hear the two options clearly.
Maya—
I'll lay out what community-led defenses require — living dunes, marsh restoration, phased volunteer mobilization.
Nyla will overlay the seasonal risk model.
Dr.
Elena Park will present the engineered option.
Then we discuss.
Nyla swivels her tablet toward the table.
The screen throws a wash of aqua across the Mayor's hands.
You hate that the data looks beautiful when it's telling you how quickly things can break.
Okay — this is the overlay against the next storm season.
Left is living defenses: initial reductions in erosion are modest but cumulative; community labor scales with time.
Right is Dr.
Elena Park's seawall: immediate drop in flood incidence for the first season, but look at the access vectors — the fishing corridors contract.
You watch Aiden Koa's jaw set.
He looks like a man holding his breath until the tide gives him an answer.
Contracting fishing corridors' — he says the phrase slowly, letting each syllable land — 'isn't just math.
It's a coastline taken out of our hands.
It's where we leave nets, where kids learn tides.
You put a wall there, you close a door to our way of living.
With respect, Aiden, those are trade-offs we can mitigate.
The engineered approach stabilizes homes immediately.
We can design access points, fish passages, adaptive bays.
The timeline buys lives.
Investors won't fund vague increments; they fund proven returns.
The word 'investors' slides through the room like a current.
You feel the old guilt rise — the night your family lost the Miller house.
You remember the sound of water in the rafters, the
way your sister's face went distant when the floor slanted.
You tell yourself that what you offer now isn't vague; it's tested ecology, it is people's labor and memory made into protection.
But 'tested' takes time.
Time is a currency the Mayor doesn't have.
We have a municipal obligation to reduce near-term risk.
If Dr.
Elena Park's model guarantees immediate flood prevention, we need that on the table.
But I also have a town to hold together politically.
Elder Fisher 1 Guarding our doors with concrete so the sea remembers us as strangers — that's a long memory.
My father fished from the same channel; you can't put a price on that.
Elder Fisher 2 And you can't ask us to build dunes where there's no labor to keep them.
People are stretched thin.
Dr.
Elena Park's clipped logic, Aiden Koa's low insistence, the elder fishers' stubborn sorrow.
Nyla's screen tracks the same month where models diverge: a single storm season where one path holds and the other fractures.
Dr.
Elena Park leans forward, hands folded like a clasped instrument.
She looks at you with a curiosity that is almost fond, but her eyes are a calculation.
Maya, your models assume a level of consistent volunteer engagement that may not hold.
After a major storm, labor drains elsewhere.
If houses flood, you lose the social capital you counted on.
My approach erects a barrier to that collapse.
Your barrier pushes the coastline seaward in an instant.
It solves flooding by displacing the shore's activities.
That displacement has cost.
We lose fishing access, marsh nurseries die — habitat services collapse and the town's livelihood is cut.
How quickly is 'quick' when the rest of us lose our way of life?
Quick isn't cruel, Maya.
Quick is survival.
I grew up where delayed action meant streets turned to rivers.
I have to weigh being right against people dying.
A silence drops like a stone.
Everyone hears the line — 'people dying' — and you feel its blunt pressure.
inevitable, ancient, indifferent to argument.
There is another layer,' she says, voice steadying.
'If we simulate the investor timeline and overlay volunteer projections, there's a window where a hybrid could reduce immediate risks while still preserving parts of access — but it's contingent.
Contingent on funding that may come with strings: oversight, timelines, clauses that hand decision rights to a board outside town.
That is the third path I keep hearing when I walk the docks.
Hybrid pilots funded by private money: they give us something to show people quickly and buy time for community measures to scale.
Politically, it's clean.
Aiden Koa slaps the table — a sharp sound that makes everyone flinch.
Politically clean doesn't mend a weathered net.
You sell that 'time' and what do we have when it's gone?
Half measures mean we get both the wall and the loss.
You don't 'buy time' with half a coastline; you mortgage it.
Elder Fisher 1 You said 'pilot' once and it turned into a highway.
We know the language of promises.
to choose one is to choose what and who survives.

'You all decide on maps.
We live the after.' Your pulse rates with the heat of a thing withheld.
Both outcomes reconverge to the ongoing council discussion.
Your fingers itch to argue the ethics of trade-offs like tide lines.
The negative press of consequences tightens around your ribs.
This is not just policy; it is the price of how the community will remember itself.

• Look directly at Aiden and speak from the personal throat
You meet his gaze.
'I don't want to take your home from you,' you say.
Your voice cracks and then steadies.
He blinks; there's a flicker — something soft and complex — but he says nothing for a beat, the old man watching like a ledger.
• Address the Mayor with policy clarity
You turn to Mayor Hale, steady the scientific cadence.
'If we model a phased living-dune implementation with contingencies for accelerated funding, here's how we keep access while scaling protection.' The mayor nods slowly, weighing the politics.
Both outcomes reconverge to the ongoing council discussion.
The room tightens; it's a drum being tightened by a slow hand.
Your sense of culpability is a live wire.
If you push for community measures and the town floods next season, you'll carry those images
forever.
If you accept engineered help and Saltwood roots are fractioned, you'll carry the faces of people who can no longer fish where their ancestors did.
Maya, you understand the optics.
Your community plan is noble — but can you guarantee volunteers when the first real storm hits?
Can you guarantee it won't be used as a political dodge?
I can't guarantee anything except that we don't trade our access for an illusion of safety.
We can build capacity if we commit.
I'm asking for trust and governance, not platitudes.
Governance is the middle word here.
We need a structure that the town can accept.
That’s why I'm proposing a conditional hybrid path: pilot engineered pieces where necessary, coupled with legally binding community oversight and stages for living defenses.
Nyla's overlay flickers again, now annotated with legal brackets and grant-time redlines.
The glowing lines of possibility look less beautiful and more like surgical diagrams.
Elder Fisher 2 And who signs those bindings?
The town clerk?
A lawyer in Portland?
Your shoulders feel like they will split under the weight.
The negative axis of the evening — doubt, guilt, fear — has been stretched taut.
Arousal spikes; you can feel the room's pulse in your throat.
The decision pressure is a rising tide.
one green and patient, one armored and immediate, one stitched in the middle that might not hold.
Each one will reconfigure who counts as neighbor, who keeps their morning routines, whose children's lessons are still on the water.
You taste the salt on the air like a premonition.
We have to decide tonight which route we take to the council and the town.
This smaller group will make a recommendation.
Maya—
He looks at you with the weary expectation of a man asking someone to take up a burden.
There are trade-offs in every option.
None are clean.
None are free.
And I'll be honest: my chest aches with the idea of choosing wrong.
Everyone watches you.
Dr.
Elena Park's face is composed, but you can see the set of her jaw quicken.
Aiden Koa's fingers curl as if around an oar.
Nyla's eyes are bright, the glow of her
tablet reflected like a lighthouse in her pupils.
Old Man Cala's silhouette is a steady cliff in the doorway, unreadable.
each catches certain things and lets others slip away.
who gets to call Saltwood 'home' after you make this call.
Mayor Jerome Hale clears his throat, and his voice is practical but edged Maya, we need a recommendation from you and this smaller council.
Decide how we steer Saltwood.
You feel the room now as the hinge it has become.
Every breath you take is the tide pulling back before a swell.
you accepted Elena’s conditional partnership.
The words are still warm in your mouth from the hall, a small, precise burn.
It should feel like relief.
Instead it feels like shifting weight — ledger lines redrawn, margins changed.
Your palms are damp on the console rail.
Metal is cool under your skin; the smell of damp electronics and brine fills the small room.
Nyla is already here, hunched over a tablet, scrolling through the
freshly negotiated oversight clauses you signed for the community advisory board.
Her pink hair is a bright flare against the gray morning.
You look like you slept with a tide chart under your pillow.' (She smirks, then softer.) 'We got the clause language in.
Oversight, transparency, phased deployment.
You did okay.
You let out a breath you didn’t know you’d been holding.
'Okay' is a compass with wobble.
We traded speed for strings.
Strings we can pull, hopefully.
Strings are better than nothing.' (She taps the screen.) 'Elena wired the first tranche overnight.

Elena steps closer, hands raised as if in mediation, but there is a flash of something like vindication at the back of her throat.
You made the call.
We'll measure, we'll mitigate.
The model will be refined.
Refined.
And meanwhile?
The quay answers with silence.
A child runs past, clutching a plastic bucket; a boat lifts its bow to the water and is hauled away on a trailer.
Someone loads a rented van with a fridge and a small mattress, and the sight is ordinary and apocalyptic at once.
• Put your hand on Aiden's shoulder and try to bridge it
You reach out, fingers tentative on his oil-worn coat.
He flinches but does not pull away; his breath is a short hit of salt and anger.
For a second you think you can feel the years between you smoothing.
Then he steps back, leaving your hand to curl empty.
• Step back and give him space, letting the quay's reality speak
You take a half-step away.
The space between you widens; it feels like giving him room to grieve, but also like conceding a small, private battlefield.
He watches you go, unreadable.
The emotional rupture remains as the town adjusts to its new boundaries; the scene continues to the cliffs.
You climb the path alone, boots making a muted slap against the wet board.
The town is quieter now; the skyline is angular with concrete where it used to be lyrical with boardwalks and boat masts.
lines drawn where none had been, a coastline remapped by consent and compromise.
a neighbor's laughter you won't hear again, the taste of
a certain clamshell chowder you will not make, the precise angle of the sun across the marsh in late July.
You think of the families who left, the men at the quay, Old Man Cala's stories that now belong to a different shore.
You think of Aiden tracing the seawall like a finger reading a scar.
You remember Elena's small smile when the investors called, and the way Mayor Hale said 'public confidence' as if it were the same thing as a beating heart.
lives saved now, incomes collapsed over seasons, tides pushed to new boundaries.
You
think of the children who will know this coastline as concrete and not as a living fold.
homes hold, insurance companies
nod, lights stay on through storms.
But the rhythms that taught people how to be Saltwood — the tide's lessons, the places where fish came close to shore, the way people said each other's names during
low-water repairs — those rhythms have been narrowed.
You think, finally, of what you will tell your own stories about this time.
Will you say you chose pragmatism and saved lives, and accept what that pragmatism took?
Will you carry regret like a stone
in your coat?
Will Aiden ever look at that concrete and hear anything of the small, messy town he grew up in?
The wind takes the question as if it were a paper, and the paper crumples.
You watch the seawall's lights flicker, and for a long time you only breathe.
There is a strange, bitter hush to the town that saved itself at the cost of its ungoverned edges.
You are not absolved.
You did what you thought was right in a moment that offered only hard choices.
The settlement stands.
The skyline is different.
Some people are safe; some are lost.
You hold the knowledge like a wound you will bandage but not remove.
You turn the tide-watch over in your hand and close your fingers around it.
The brass is familiar; it has been at every decision point.
Tonight it feels almost too warm.
You walk down the cliff path, the lights of the seawall receding, and you carry with you the narrow shore that remains — clean, defended, and diminished.
THE END
